coredata.dc:title: Advances in carbon nanomaterials for immunotherapy
coredata.dc:creator: Azevedo, Silvana
Costa-Almeida, Raquel
Santos, Susana G.
Magalhães, Fernão D.
Pinto, Artur M.
coredata.dc:description: Remarkable advances have been achieved in the field of nanomedicine and immunotherapy, since it was found that several nanomaterials can modulate the immune system. The main objective of this review is to collect and discuss studies where carbon nanomaterials (CNM), due to their unique structure and composition, revealed to be important tools in cancer treatment, immunostimulation, and immunosuppression of inflammatory or autoimmune diseases. These materials offer several advantages, such as being effective platforms for drug delivery and targeting, immunomodulation, phototherapy, and others. Some effects still need to be upgraded and some questions persist. Positive results were already achieved, although translation to clinics is still needed. The evolution of future investigations may still revolutionize the field of biomedicine.

Immunotherapy
Immune cells

Cancer

This work was financed by FEDER funds through the COMPETE 2020 - Operational Programme for Competitiveness and Internationalisation (POCI), Portugal 2020, and by national funds (PIDDAC) through FCT/MCTES in the framework of the project POCI-01–0145-FEDER-031143, NovaDerma - New platforms for dermatological phototherapy (PTDC/BTM-MAT/31143/2017). Artur Pinto thanks the Portuguese Foundation for Science and Technology (FCT) for the financial support of his work contract through the Scientific Employment Stimulus - Individual Call – [CEECIND/03908/2017].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CNM most relevant properties and modifications and their effect on dendritic cells and macrophages. CNM with different structures, surface chemistry, sizes and shapes can be functionalized with various biomolecules (drugs, peptides, antibodies, DNA/RNA, and others). These structural differences provide specificity to the nanomaterials and can be tailored according to the desired immunomodulatory effect, towards immunosuppression or immunostimulation. Immunosuppression will lead to increased anti-inflammatory cytokines production and/or inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines, resulting in a reduction of immune cell activation, which is advantageous for the treatment of autoimmune, auto-inflammatory and allergic diseases. Immunostimulation leads to the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and reactive oxygen species (ROS) and/or to activation of the adaptive immunity (B and T cells) by CNM, resulting in antibody production and specific killing of tumour cells or pathogens. Created with BioRender.com.

Representation of the structural characteristics of different CNM – Graphene, Multi-walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNT), Single-walled Carbon Nanotubes (SWCNT), Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD), Nanodiamonds (ND), Carbon Dots (CD), Fullerenes, and classification according to their dimensions and carbon hybridization. Dimension was categorized considering their size or grain size, being 0D the nanomaterials with all dimensions below 100 nm, 1D the nanomaterials with 1 dimension above 100 nm, and 2D the nanomaterials with 2 dimensions greater than 100 nm. Created with BioRender.com and adapted from [38].

CNM interactions with tumour cells. CNM, such as SWCNT and GBM combined with NIR irradiation can target and kill tumour cells through the local increase in temperature. [67,68] Also, some GBM, like graphene oxide, functionalized with anti-cancer drugs or other biomolecules can activate the immune cells (macrophages, dendritic cells, T cells…) to kill tumour cells. [74] The release of tumour antigens, induce APC (Antigen Presenting Cells) to process and present antigens that will be recognized by certain lymphocytes, such as T cells, leading to their immunoactivation. Also, autologous T cells can be engineered and reprogrammed (CAR T cells) to improve their function and destroy the cancer cells after being administered through injection [58,61]. Created with BioRender.com. CNM: Carbon Nanomaterials; GBM: Graphene Based Materials; GQD: Graphene Quantum Dots; MWCNT: Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotubes; ND: Nanodiamonds; NIR: Near Infrared; SWCNT: Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes; Cytokines: IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2, IL-6 and IL-12.

Examples of cancer immunotherapeutic modulation induced by the presence of CNM. (A) MWCNT conjugated to tumour lysate protein (H22P) were applied as tumour-cell vaccines (TCV) to treat liver cancer. Black arrow in (a) points to the place where a tumour used to grow but shrank to a minimal residue in TCV+CNT-H22P group, while the tumour in the control group without both CNT and H22P (arrow in b) expanded considerably; cure rates for the different conditions tested (c). [89] ©2008 Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim (B) Effects of GO-PEG (GP) combined with NIR on the induction of macrophage polarization on osteosarcoma growth and invasion. The inhibitory effect of GP + NIR on invasion and migration ability of HOS cells via macrophage polarization. (a,b); Images of an explanted tumour 1 and 2 days after NIR photothermal therapy (PTT) using a laser (808 nm; 0.7 W cm−2) for 90 s combined with GP (injected subcutaneously, 0.05 mg mL−1 of GO), and control tumours (c); temperature curve measured during PTT in vivo for the different conditions tested (d); tumour volume variation during the experiment (10 days, GP was added at day 4) (e) [96] © 2020 Elsevier B.V. (C) Dendritic cells (DC) viability after being exposed to different concentrations of GO–PEG–PEI conjugates with variable PEI molecular weights (a) and their influence on DC maturation (b); Effect of intradermally injected GO–PEG–PEI (10 kDa), those modified with Urease B (Ure B), and controls, in mice sera levels of IL-17 (c) and TNF-α (d). [97] © The Royal Society of Chemistry 2016 Alum, Aluminium; CNT, Carbon Nanotubes; GO, Graphene Oxide; PEG, Polyethylene glycol; PEI, polyethylenimine; TVC, Tumour Cell Vaccines.

Effect of graphene oxide (GO) and vanillin-reduced graphene oxide (V-rGO) on the level of cytokines and chemokines (IL-1β, IL-8, TNF-α, GM-CSF, IL-6, and MCP-1). Human leukaemia monocytic cells (THP-1) were treated with GO (50 µg mL−1) and V-rGO (50 µg mL−1) for 24 h. Bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) (1 µg mL−1) was used as a positive control for inflammation. Both GO and V-rGO revealed to lead to significant increase in the secretion of pro-inflamatory cytokines, such as IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α and GM-CSF, and the chemokine, MCP-1. [100] © 1996–2022 MDPI.

Immunostimulatory action induced by CNM, including immune cells activation and pro-inflammatory cytokine production. CNM can exert a direct effect on cells, inducing ROS generation and activating immune cells, triggering an inflammatory response, with increased pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IFN- γ, IL-1β, IL-6, amongst others) release. CNM can be further modified using specific drugs, polymers or antibodies which results in an indirect activation of antigen-presenting cells, also resulting in an inflammatory/immune response. [115],[116] Created with BioRender.com. CNM, Carbon Nanomaterials; NIR, Near-Infra Red; ROS, Reactive Oxygen Species.

CNM induced immunostimulation. A. Evaluation of the MWCNT capability to contradict stimulated space flight immunosuppression. a) Microgravity (M) was compared with static controls (OX-MWCNT-NH3+ alone or in the presence of cell activators, such as ConA). Activation marker assay of T cells: CD25 (left) and CD69 (right) expression was assessed by flow cytometry on CD3+ T cells present on PBMCs. ConA (10 µg mL−1) was used as positive control. b) Cytokine secretion assay (TNF-α, IL-10, IFN-γ, IL-6, IL-13 and IL-2). Cytokine production was assessed by multiplex ELISA on PBMCs and on isolated human T lymphocytes. [122] © The Royal Society of Chemistry 2014 B Plasmodium vivax detection in the serum of the tested mice by immunofluorescence. a) and b) Mice immunized with a non-relevant peptide (33,189). c) and d) Mice immunized with Plasmodium vivax AMA-1 peptide 21267. e) and f) Mice immunized with the CNT-21267 peptide complex. g) and h) Mice immunized with just CNT. i) and j) Placebo group: Mice immunized with a saline solution. [123] © 2008 Elsevier Ltd. ConA, Concanavalin A; LPS, Lipopolysaccharide MWCNT, Multi-walled Carbon Nanotube, PBMCs Peripheral Blood Mononuclear Cells.

Immunologic effects of Fullerenol based DNA vaccines. The formulations were obtained by mixing envelope protein (Env) DNA with fullerenol (Fullerenol-Env). The DNA antigen is encapsulated during the self-assembly of fullerenol. The concentration of IL-2, TNF-α and IFN-γ were determined 14 days after mice immunization by intradermal injection with Fullerenol-Env formulation at different doses (0.8–20, 4–20 and 20–20 μg per mouse). It resulted in a significant increase in TNF-α and IFN-γ production, revealing the induction of a Th1 response. [128] © 2013 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.

Immunosuppressive response induced by CNM through immune cells and pro-inflammatory cytokine production inhibition, and anti-inflammatory cytokines release. CNM, such as fullerenes can inhibit ROS formation, suppressing the immune cells activation (inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines release). [134] When functionalized with specific drugs or polymers, CNM can also suppress immune cells activation, and produce anti-inflammatory cytokines release. [120],[132] Created with BioRender.com. CNM, Carbon Nanomaterials; ROS, Reactive Oxygen Species. Cytokines: IL-4, IL-10, IL-11, IL-13, IFN-γ and TNF-α.

CNM induced immunosuppression. A. Uptake of MWCNT by monocytes, seen in transmission electronic microscopy images (a). Effect of increasing concentrations of MWCNT on PBMC viability (assessed by trypan blue exclusion assay after 48 h of culture) (b). Secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines, TNF-α and IL-6, by PBMCs from healthy donors after a 48 h stimulation with or without LPS 1 µg mL−1 in the presence or absence of the referred concentrations of MWCNT (c). Secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 by Toll-like receptor agonist-stimulated PBMCs from healthy donors after a 48 h stimulation in the presence or absence of MWCNT (d) [130] © 2012 Elsevier Ireland Ltd. B. Representative hematoxylin and eosin–stained lung sections at equivalent magnifications from a control animal (a to c) and after inhalation of 5 mg/m3 MWCNT for 14 consecutive days (d to f). [136] © 2007, Oxford University Press AD, alveolar duct, MWCNT, Multi-walled Carbon Nanotubes, PBMC, Peripheral Blood Mononuclear Cells, TB, terminal bronchial; TLR, Toll-like receptor; V, vessel.

Transmission electron microscopy images of (GQD) graphene quantum dots (100 μg mL−1) internalization in different types of cells after 1 h of incubation. The cells tested were Jurkat T cells, J774 macrophages, and HepG2, a human hepatoma cell line. The arrows indicate GQD presence. [142] © 2014 American Chemical Society.
Carbon nanomaterials and their characteristics (described in more detail on Supplementary Materials). Images were adapted from BioRender.com).

∼ 1 nm diameter with a crystalline form of carbon

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Remarkable advances have been achieved in the field of nanomedicine and immunotherapy, since it was found that several nanomaterials can modulate the immune system. The main objective of this review is to collect and discuss studies where carbon nanomaterials (CNM), due to their unique structure and composition, revealed to be important tools in cancer treatment, immunostimulation, and immunosuppression of inflammatory or autoimmune diseases. These materials offer several advantages, such as being effective platforms for drug delivery and targeting, immunomodulation, phototherapy, and others. Some effects still need to be upgraded and some questions persist. Positive results were already achieved, although translation to clinics is still needed. The evolution of future investigations may still revolutionize the field of biomedicine.

ce:section.ce:para: The ability to activate or suppress an immune response, generally designated as immunomodulation, is important for different areas of medicine, such as cancer, allergy, autoimmune diseases, transplantation and infectious diseases [1–5]. The immune system identifies and neutralizes foreign pathogens and materials, that may threaten our health, and then induces that response to allow returning to homoeostasis. However, the immune system can be disturbed in several ways, resulting in undesired suppression and inability to fight pathogens or cancer; or overstimulation, potentially leading to chronic activation and tissue damage. There is increasing biomedical research about different immunomodulatory strategies to better understand the mechanisms underlying immune system responses caused by specific compounds and take profits from that modulation to promote immunity (e.g., to fight cancer) or tolerance (e.g., to resolve autoimmunity) [6,7]. One of those strategies is using nanomaterials with capacity to stimulate or inhibit an inflammatory response. The natural reaction of the innate immune system to the direct recognition of a foreign body is a non-specific inflammation. Macrophages and dendritic cells (DC) are cells of the innate immune system that have important roles in inflammation and immune response. Macrophages are cells of the innate immune system, very important in the inflammatory process, since they phagocyte dead cells, pathogens and debris (and also nanoparticles), secrete a plethora of pro- and anti-inflammatory mediators that can initiate the inflammatory cascade, and may present antigens to T cells, activating the adaptive immune system. Macrophages are key cells in the host response to biomaterials and medical devices, which is conditioned by their physical and chemical properties. On the other hand, the main function of DC is to survey the periphery and present antigens to T cells. DC are the most potent antigen-presenting cells, coordinate the communication between innate and adaptive immune systems, triggering immune stimulation or immune suppression [8]. Lymphocytes (Natural Killer (NK), T and B cells) may be recruited to fight against threats, including pathogens or tumour cells. In cancer, for instance, cytotoxic T and NK lymphocytes, release cytokines, blockers of cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4 (CTLA-4) and programmed cell death protein 1/programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-1/PD-L1) inhibitors, to directly kill tumour cells [9,10]. On the other hand, prolonged or uncontrolled immunostimulation, can lead to or exacerbate chronic inflammation, autoimmunity and allergic reactions [11,12].

Over the last few years, evidence has been mounting supporting the potential of nanomaterials to modulate the immune response. Biomaterials alone, or conjugated with specific biomolecules, can be used to directly modulate the immune system, consequently offering new approaches for the enhancement of immune-based therapies against inflammatory and autoimmune diseases, as well as cancer [13–15]. Nanoparticles have been used for various health applications, like in diagnostics, imaging, and as drug delivery systems. Due to their high surface area they can extensively interact with other molecules, which can be used to modulate immune cells towards pro or anti-inflammatory purposes [16]. Since they are foreign to the host organism, several nanoparticles induce an immune system response and have been increasingly explored for applications in immunotherapy. Nanoparticles can present anti-tumour properties or ideal characteristics to be used as therapeutic agents or vaccine adjuvants. Their size, surface charge, functionalization, hydrophobicity or hydrophilicity, and capability to bind to diverse biomolecules are critical factors that define the biocompatibility between the nanoparticle and the target immune cells [17]. Nanomaterials can be designed to imitate specific foreign signals and consequently manipulate the adaptive immune response, providing new biological systems to be used in the field of immunotherapy [18,19]. However, it has also been shown that there are characteristics of the nanoparticles that can have relevant immunotoxic effects, such as activation of stress-related genes, membrane disruption, and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines [20,21]. Therefore, a thoughtful approach is needed to select and design materials with desired properties for immunotherapy.

Nanoparticles modified by adsorption or cross-linking of biomolecules or antibodies can identify and target specific cell types, including tumour cells. Silica particles or metal-derived nanoparticles have been traditionally used, since they can directly or indirectly, through antibodies or drugs attached to them, induce a desired immune response. However, metal nanoparticles, such as gold nanoparticles, have been shown to be nondegradable, ultimately leading to bioaccumulation. Silica nanoparticles have been reported to require surface functionalization toward improving their biocompatibility, being their effect on immune cells dependant on their size, structure and other physical properties [22]. On the other hand, carbon nanomaterials (CNM) often are biocompatible even without functionalization, and commonly they are easily degradable or excreted from the human body. Additionally, CNM can provide a multiversity of possibilities for surface functionalization, when comparing with other nanomaterials [23–25]. CNM embrace a large variety of versatile carbon atom structures, that can be easily tunned for immunotherapy by varying their physico-chemical properties, such as size, degree of oxidation, and surface functional groups. Carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene-based materials, fullerenes and others, when alone or combined with therapeutic agents, can induce immune responses, useful for the treatment or prevention of several diseases [26–29]. While the picture of the CNM interactions with the immune system remains incomplete, several advantages have been widely reported, including their ability to cross biological barriers (e.g., BBB), ability to act as immunomodulatory agents by themselves and serve as drug carriers, and capacity to respond to external stimuli, like radiation [30,31]. There are several promising non- carbon-based materials for immunotherapy, such as inorganic quantum dots which have been reviewed recently elsewhere [32–34]. Conversely, the focus of this review will be on CNM.

Recently, CNM have gained huge attention in the biomedical scientific community, and the results obtained regarding immune system modulation are increasingly satisfactory. Due to their exceptional properties and capacity to present variable sizes, shapes, surface chemistry, low cytotoxicity, and ability to attach different biomolecules, drugs or antibodies, the use of these nanomaterials became very promising. The main mechanisms of immunomodulation (immunosuppression and immunostimulation) that can be elicited by CNM are presented in Fig. 1 , with particular focus on the response mediated by macrophages and dendritic cells.

Currently available review literature is focused on nanomaterials other than CNM, as for example inorganic nanoparticles. Only short sections are dedicated to carbon nanomaterials, within which most attention is given to CNT, and graphene is briefly mentioned [32–36]. This review will congregate and discuss, for the first time, the most relevant information available presently, regarding the use of different types of CNM in immunotherapy. The main characteristics of CNM and their effects in the immune system will be presented. The first part will comprise studies regarding cancer immunotherapy. The second part will focus on immunomodulation therapies, for diseases other than cancer. It is divided in immunostimulation and immunosuppression. (Fig. 1). In the third and final part, some challenges and questions that still remain related to the side effects of proposed immunotherapies will be highlighted, together with future prospects.

Carbon atoms contain six electrons, arranged in the 1s22s22p2 electronic configuration. Their unique properties and high plasticity allow them to form different covalent bonds with other carbon atoms or elements which empower their presence from small organic molecules to complex structures such as CNM with distinct and remarkable properties. Fig. 2 depicts the classification of CNM according to their dimensions, hybridization and crystalinity. CNM can be divided into 0D nanomaterials, which include graphene quantum dots (GQD), nanodiamonds (ND), carbon dots (CD), and fullerenes, 1D nanomaterials comprising different types of carbon nanotubes (CNT), and 2D nanomaterials, like graphene (G) and its derivatives. Also, CNM can present variable amounts of sp2 and sp3 carbon bonds, diverse ranges of crystallinity, and can be chemically modified in numerous ways, making them outstanding tuneable platforms for immunotherapy [37]. This section provides background on the different CNM types and on their most relevant properties, which are presented on Table 1, but also on Supplementary Materials more extensively. Functionalization strategies and immunotherapeutic approaches will be addressed more detailedly in the following sections.

Oncological malignancies affect worldwide population with a yearly incidence of around 18 million new cancer cases and almost 10 million cancer deaths each year, making it the second leading cause of death globally [51]. Early detection and selective treatment strategies are paramount to fight this disease effectively. The most common treatments include surgical tumour excision, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, or combinations thereof. Tumour removal is invasive, leaving potentially large defects in the tissue, and not able to remove all cancer cells, often requiring a combination with chemo, radio or immunotherapies. Systemic chemotherapy has severe side effects, affecting healthy cells, debilitating the entire organism and potentially leading to the development of resistance. Radiotherapy is often performed after surgical tumour removal, but its side effects include cardiovascular complications, especially when used to treat thoracic cancers (e.g. lung, breast) for example. On the other hand, immunotherapy approaches stem from the perception that while immune cells can kill abnormal tumour cells, tumours can turn off immune responses and promote the development of tolerogenic immune cells that favour tumour growth and metastasis [52–55]. Fig. 3 represents the main CNM interactions with tumour cells. In general, anti-cancer immunotherapy consists of a stimulation of the immune response against the tumour. This was initially achieved by the administration of cytokines like tumour necrosis factor (TNF)-α and interleukin (IL)−2, that are naturally produced in response to pathogens and can activate several immune cell populations, like macrophages, T and NK lymphocytes [56]. However, their short half-life and the need for repeated high doses can cause severe secondary effects. Antibodies that target checkpoint inhibitors, like CTLA4 and PD-1 or its ligand PD-L1, are still widely researched for cancer therapy [10]. They allow for T cells to remain active, recognize and kill tumour cells. The engineering of autologous T cells to express chimaeric antigen receptor (CAR) that are tumour-specific has also been approved for clinical use, although currently is only used for melanoma. These engineered T cells are then re-administered to the patient, where they recognize and kill tumour cells [57]. Although patients are reported to achieve remission and improved survival, their long-term effects are yet unknown, and particularly in solid tumours, combination therapies are required to improve the survival of CAR T cells [58]. NK cells can target tumour cells and release granules and granzymes that cause tumour cells lysis. As NK cells do not often infiltrate solid tumours, their use in cancer immunotherapies has been mainly focused on hematopoietic malignancies [59,60]. The adaptive immune response against tumours can be triggered by activated antigen presenting cells (APC), like macrophages and DC. Autologous DC are the most widely researched for anti-cancer vaccines, whether by exposure to cancer antigens or engineering them to express those antigens [61]. These cells are then re-injected into the patient and can specifically activate T cells against the tumour [62]. Cancer vaccines based on the delivery of exogenous nucleic acids, RNA or DNA to APC, toward triggering an immune response against tumours, are also being tested in several clinical trials [63,64]. The success of immunotherapies still depends on several factors, like the tumour immunogenicity as well as, the choosing of the best pathway, bearing in mind that it should be the one that most benefits the interaction between, cells, proteins and antibodies, in order to more efficiently fight against the tumour and avoiding side effects [65,66].

Over the last years, the number of studies in cancer immunotherapy field involving the use of CNM has been considerably increasing. Diverse strategies have been reported, including modulation of the immune system due to their intrinsic characteristics and/or functionalization, targeted drug delivery to enhance the efficacy of chemotherapy, and their functionalization with antibodies to allow a selective accumulation in tumours, which might be followed by hyperthermia therapy using near infrared (NIR) radiation, owing to the intrinsic high NIR absorption exhibited by some CNM, like CNT and GBM [67,68]. CNM can be administered inside the tumours or be targeted by surface modification, localizing the heating effect in cancer cells, and decreasing damage to surrounding tissues. Also, drug release can be triggered by NIR irradiation of CNM/drug conjugates, increasing treatment specificity and efficacy [69,70]. Some mild hyperthermia strategies with low energy irradiation, using light emitting diodes (LED) or low power lasers, have been shown to increase treatment selectivity even further, when compared with thermal ablation strategies, being an alternative to more invasive cancer phototherapies [71–73]. CNM specific functionalization and size have been reported to influence immune cells, inducing specific activation of antigen presenting cells, regulating the immune response against tumours through the stimulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines secretion. To improve targeting, those nanomaterials can be functionalized with drugs or antibodies, which can be further combined with polymers, like polyethylene glycol (PEG), that adds stability to the nanomaterials, can improve treatment specificity and potentiate the anti-tumour immune response [74]. PEG has also shown to provide efficient targeting and protective coating on particles for immune evasion [75,76]. The conjugation of anti-cancer drugs with CNM allows their preferential accumulation in the tumour site, due to the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, allowing the reduction of therapeutic doses required, thus minimizing side effects [77]. CNM size and surface properties can be adjusted to maximize cell uptake in a targeted way, by varying the molecules attached at their surface [78–81].

CNM can also boost vaccines efficacy, through their functionalization, enhancing the anti-cancer immune response, by promoting biosafety and by optimizing immunization routes. They can deliver the totality or a part of the tumour cell which provides tumour antigens as targets, triggering cell death by priming antitumor immune actions [82,83]. Table 2 . condenses studies where CNM, with or without functionalization, were applied in cancer immunotherapy field and in anti-cancer vaccination.

Anti-HER2 antibody can recognize human endothelial receptor 2 (HER2), which is commonly overexpressed in several cancer cells, enabling to specifically target them. Xiao et al. reported the use of oxidized SWCNT activated by the introduction of carboxylic groups and covalently functionalization with anti-HER2 antibody. This complex allowed to target breast carcinoma cells and consequently allowed their thermal ablation using NIR irradiation [109]. Villa et al. covalently attached WT1Pep427 on solubilized SWCNT scaffolds, in order to improve the immune response against tumour antigens. The internalization, in a first phase, occurred through their uptake in peripheral vesicles and then, transportation to a perinuclear area. Although no differences were found between the peptide-CNT conjugates and the peptide alone in vitro, conjugates were internalized by cells in vivo. Additionally, in the presence of an immunological adjuvant (titer Max), it induced over 70% of peptide-specific IgG response, comparing with the peptide and/or the SWCNT alone [84]. Zhao et al. conjugated functionalized SWCNT with CpG oligodeoxynucleotides, observing SWCNT uptake in vitro and in vivo, in intracranial GL261 gliomas. The CpG delivery mediated by mouse primary monocytes was potentiated by SWCNT and resulted in the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such tumour necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) and IL-12. Intracranial GL261 gliomas elimination was observed in 50% of the mice treated with low doses of SWCNT-CpG by intracranial injection, while free CpG and free SWCNT did not present any effect. Additionally, animals that survived the tumour , did not present tumour recurrence after treatment with SWCNT-CpG in a follow-up of more than 3 months, which can indicate long-term immunity [85]. functionalized-SCWNT, leading to an innovative strategy for lymphocytes stimulation. Such approach can be used in situations that require a strong stimulus for an effective expansion of T cells outside the body for several clinical applications, for instance, in cancer treatment [86]. In a subsequent study using the same functionalized nanomaterial, Fadel et al. observed increased IL-2 and IFN- γ production by T cells, comparing to free antibodies, suggesting that SWCNT can act as efficient substrate to be adopted in cancer immunotherapy [87]. Some years later, in consequence of the results previously obtained, the use of CNT as a substrate for antigen-specific response to a multimeric T cell stimulus with MHC-I protein has been demonstrated. Results showed an enhancement of T cell response through the increase of IFN- γ levels and an active way for presenting MHC-I, proving that engineered complexes with CNT can be adapted to efficiently respond to immunomodulatory needs [88].

Meng et al. produced MWCNT/H22P tumour lysate protein conjugates, being the latter a cocktail of tumour proteins alternative to tumour-specific antigens. MWCNT/H22P influence on the immune system toward developing a tumour-cell vaccine to hepatocarcinoma was evaluated. An enhancement on the antitumour cell response and a 55 % improvement of the cure rate of H22 liver cancer were observed in mice (Figure 4. A) [89]. Some years later, they validated the results using MWCNT alone on an H22 tumour-bearing mouse model and showing more than 80 % improvement in the production of inflammatory cytokines, namely interferon-γ (IFN-γ) and some ILs and a significant increase of tumour growht inhibition rate [90]. Also, Mocan et al. used MWCNT (0.1 mg mL−1) combined with Embryonic Stem Cells (ESC) to induce anticancer effects in a mouse model. An enhanced antitumour response was observed with increased CD4+ and CD8+ lymphocytes percentages, and higher expression of IL-2 and IFN-γ in the ESC-MWCNT treated group. Tumour weight and volume decreased over 80%, comparing to results obtained when only ESC, MWCNT or none of them were administered [91]. Hassan et al. studied the possibility of modulating the immune response against tumour cells. They used MWCNT functionalized with an amine-terminated spacer,
