+ OVA-CpG- α CD40 incorporated on MWCNT and, consequently, the inhibition of the OVA expressing melanoma cells [61]. Čolić et al. conjugated a selective endosomal Toll-like receptor (TLR) 7, 7-TOG, to oxidized MWCNT and analyzed their ability to activate monocyte-derived cells (MoDC). The nanocomplex 7-TOG+MWCNT upregulated CD86 expression and enhanced T helper polarizing capability. Authors reported a partially mature phenotype of MoDC and attributed this effect to the cellular uptake of 7-TOG+MWCNT complex, which led to intracellular accumulation of 7-TOG. A similar effect was obtained with 10-fold higher amounts of soluble 7-TOG, suggesting a role for MWCNT functionalized with TLR7 agonists in improving immunostimulatory approaches using DC-based vaccines [92].

Luo et al. observed that photoluminescent CD can improve antigen uptake and accelarate the maturation of DC. When combined with Ovalbumin (CD-OVA), increases were observed in the expression of costimulatory molecules CD80 and CD86, and on the production of TNF-α from DC. Also, the CD-OVA complex, allowed to induce the expression of IFN-γ and, in vivo, it demonstrated to reduce the progression of melanoma cancer in mice [93]. Recently, Liang et al. developed a novel procedure consisting in composing cancer cells with CD (CM-cancer) for cancer immunotherapy. The CM-cancer recruited and activated macrophages and DC, then delivering the cancer antigens into the lymph nodes to activate cytotoxic T cells, leading to a whole body immune response that resulted in specific killing of primary and metastatic tumors in mice [94]. Finally, Kim et al. studied the effect of chlorin e6 (Ce6)-loaded pH sensitive CD on anti-cancer immune response under pH-controlled conditions. They demonstrated that Ce6 release was higher at tumoral pH (6.5), which resulted in superior singlet oxygen generation. When combined with laser irradiation (671 nm), it resulted in a large amount of activated CD8+ T, natural killer, and mature dendritic cells recruitment into the tumour, and in about 4-fold decreased tumour growth, even at untreated sites [95].

Deng et al. studied the combination of GO-PEG with NIR irradiation in vitro using human osteosarcoma (HOS) and macrophage cell lines, as well as in vivo for applications in PTT for osteosarcoma treatment through the inhibition of M2 polarization as an anti-tumour therapeutic target. Herein, authors explored PTT potential to modulate tumour-associated macrophages and demonstrated that NIR irradiation (808 nm, 0.7 W cm−2 for 2 min) of RAW264.2 cell line in the presence of GO-PEG resulted in decreased expression of CD206, suggesting an impairment of M2 polarization of macrophages. Afterwards, macrophage conditioned media were collected and used to evaluate the migration and invasion capacity of HOS cells. Strikingly, HOS cultured with macrophage conditioned media collected upon treatment with GO-PEG and NIR irradiation were unable to repair the scratch in a wound healing in vitro assay after 24 h, as opposed to controls. Similar results were obtained using a transwell invasion assay, demonstrating that macrophages treated with GO-PEG and NIR may have an altered secretome, which inhibits tumour cell invasion and migration. In vivo studies demonstrated tumour weight/volume decreased by more than 50% in the presence of GO-PEG (0.05 mg mL−1) when NIR irradiated (808 nm; 0.7 W cm−2) for 90 s (Fig. 4, B). Immunohistological analysis revealed that CD206 expression was reduced in tumours treated with GO-PEG and NIR irradiation, whereas higher levels of arginase-1 were detected, supporting preferential macrophage differentiation toward M1 phenotype [96]. Xu et al. idealized a highly effective GO-based vaccine nano-adjuvant, using the urease B model antigen. GO was single (GO–PEG and GO–PEI) and double (GO–PEG–PEI) coated with PEG and polyethyleneimine (PEI) polymers to enhance its surface properties. In vitro studies showed that GO-PEG-PEI induced the activation of TLR2 and TLR4 signalling pathways in DC and the delivery of Ure B with low toxicity. In vivo experiments showed the potential of this complex to be used as vaccine adjuvant, since GO-PEG-PEI-Ure B improved T cell proliferation comparing to free Ure B (Fig. 4, C) [97].

Also, Wang et al. engineered a GO nano-based system (GO-AlO(OH)), by adding aluminium sulfate through chemical precipitation onto GO nanosheets (2 mg mL−1), in order to deliver OVA and elicit an immune response. In vitro studies showed a 50% increased OVA uptake with the GO-Al complex, together with an increase of CD86, MHC-I and MHC-II expression in murine bone marrow cells, indicating immune cell activation. Furthermore, in vivo assays showed an increase of T lymphocyte response and more than 70% decrease in tumour weight [98]. A similar approach was tested by Yue et al., that attached OVA to GO and assessed the effects in bone marrow DC. An increase in OVA uptake was observed after 12 h and also, the expression of co-stimulatory CD86, and MHC molecules, MHC-I and MHC-II, were enhanced with the complex OVA+GO. The tests in E.G7 tumour-bearing mice showed a relevant decrease in tumour volume [99]. Particularly, Gurunathan et al. conducted a study to evaluate GO and vanillin-reduced GO (V-rGO) toxicity towards THP-1, a leukaemia-derived monocyte/macropahge cell line. Both GO and V-rGO at a concentration of 100 µg mL−1 induced a decrease in THP-1 cells viability of 40% and 20%, respectively. These materials induced oxidative stress in THP-1 cells, as demonstrated by a redox imbalance with increased levels of malondialdehyde and decreased levels of glutathione, glutathione peroxidase, superoxide dismutase and catalase, thereby accelerating apoptosis observed through up-regulation of apoptosis-related genes (p53, p21, Bax, caspase-9, and caspase-3). Furthermore, GO and V-rGO induced the production of several cytokines and/or chemokines, such as IL-1β, TNF-α, GM-CSF, IL-6, IL-8, and MCP-1, which was more pronounced in cells cultured with V-rGO, as compared to GO. The activation of pro-inflammatory pathways (cytokines release and ROS generation), which are potentiated with the vanillin functionalization and the oxygen groups reduction, could help to fight leukaemia (Fig. 5 ) [100].

Sui et al. combined GQD with cisplatin (CDDP) to study its cellular and nuclear uptake aiming at increasing the efficacy of chemotherapy and overcome drug resistance. GQD improved the interaction of CDDP with DNA (CDDP normally forms a covalent adduct with DNA, that inhibits its replication and RNA transcription). Additionally, GQD increased DNA fragmentation, leading to an increase in cell death. However, the affinity of GQD to DNA requires further investigation, since the mechanisms that cause that interaction are not completely understood [101]. The use of GQD was also studied by Tomić et al. to induce autophagic-dependant suppression of pro-inflammatory T cells through the activation of tolerogenic DC. Non-toxic doses of GQD (< 200 µg mL−1) inhibited the production of T helper cytokines and increased the stimulation of Th2 cytokines, such as IL-10, IL-4 and IL-5 [102]. Likewise, Qin et al. investigated the effect of GQD in the immune response and found that GQD increased ROS production, autophagy, and the inflammatory response of activated macrophages. Also, while in concentrations as low as 10 µg mL−1, GQD increased in more than 50% the production of cytokines, such as TNF-α and IL-8, higher concentrations (100 and 200 µg mL−1) induced opposite effects, reinforcing the importance of using the right doses of CNM to elicit immunosuppressive or immunostimulatory responses. Also, macrophage apoptosis increased by more than 60% at the concentration of 200 µg mL−1 [103].

Li et al. studied the delivery of doxorubicin conjugated with polyglycerol nanodiamonds (Nano-DOX) using dendritic cells as carriers (Nano-DOX-loaded DC) to treat glioblastoma. This complex proved to maintain the viability of THP-1-derived DC in vitro, enhanced the release of IFN-γ when in concentrations from 40 to 400 µg mL−1, comparing to DC only control, and up-regulated CD69 expression by ∼25% in
CD
4
+
CD
8 +
lymphocytes. The combination with carboxyfluorescein diacetate succinimidyl ester (CSFE), which provides fluorescence, allowed labelling the glioblastoma cells in all tumour-bearing mice. The activation of mDC (mouse bone marrow-derived DC) and lymphocytes was observed, through the decay of fluorescence [104]. In a similar approach, Suarez-Kelly et al. stimulated NK cells and monocytes with fluorescent ND (FND, 100 µg mL−1), resulting in FND uptake and in an enhancement (more than 50%) of CD69 expression, comparing with the control without nanomaterials, an indicative of the activation of NK cells. In an animal model, FND were clearly visualized, allowing the targeting of carcionagenic cells since the FND were uptaken by them [105].

Zhang et al. constructed a nanodiamond-based system to deliver paclitaxel (PTX-DNA@ND), with the objective of targeting breast cancer cells and improve treatment efficacy. PTX-DNA@ND conjugation with monoclonal antibodies (mAbs), allowed tumour cell targeting. Also, the uptake of the nanodiamond-base complex increased the fluorescent signal in MDA-MB-231 cells, comparing with free PTX and the complex without the antibodies. A significant (> 60%) cell death was observed using low concentrations of PTX-DNA/mAb@ND [106]. Zhu et al. explored the possibility of using the C60(OH)x (average x = 24) fullerol as a new immunomodulatory approach in hepatocarcinoma. C60 (OH)x enhanced the inherent immunity of tumour-bearing mice by potentiating peritoneal macrophages phagocytosis both in vitro and in vivo. Additionally, surrounding fibroblasts and lymphocytes activity was enhanced in consequence of TNF-α increase induced by C60(OH)x, which inhibited tumour growth to the neighbour tissues [107]. Similar effects have been observed by Liu et al., with efficient anti-tumour activity being found in vivo, and immunomodulatory effects like increased pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and INF-γ) produced by macrophages and lymphocytes treated with C60(OH)20, as well as an increased CD4+/CD8+ ratio, demonstrating immune cells stimulation [108].

Overall, CNM large surface area, small size, and chemical versatility has been shown to facilitate the conjugation with diverse molecules, including drugs, antibodies or molecules recognized by the target cells. Together with the enhanced permeability and retention effect that can be achieved for materials with adequate small sizes, this makes them some of the most advanced and promising platforms presently available for targeted immunotherapy. Furthermore, CNM such as CNT and GBM present the ability to absorb radiation in the NIR region, resulting in localized temperature increase, which can lead to an increase in cancer cells membrane permeability. Incubating cancer cells with SWCNT concentrations, in general, inferior to 1 mg mL−1 for time periods around 24 h and further NIR irradiation (< 4 min) resulted in 90–95% cell death [96,109–111]. MWCNT, in concentrations inferior to 1 mg mL−1, revealed to increase IFN-γ levels in vitro and to reduce hepatoma tumour volume in vivo [61,91]. Due to its small size (from 50 to 2000 nm), GO (in concentrations below 0.5 mg mL−1) has been shown to facilitate cellular uptake of drugs loaded at its surface. Drug functionalization of GO with OVA and Ure B, for instance, allowed a better targeting comparing with non-functionalized GO as demonstrated by in vitro studies (less than 48 h of incubation). Simultaneously, it was revealed over than 50% decrease in tumour volume in several cases, highlighting the anti-tumour effect of GO. GQD, fullerenes and ND, all at concentrations inferior to 200 µg mL−1 and without any functionalization, have been shown to induce an immune response, mainly by the stimulation of ILs and TNF-α pathways, since the immune system recognizes them as strange bodies. In general, all carbon-derived materials referred above demonstrate to have the capacity to modulate and influence immune response directly or indirectly, through specific antibodies attached to them or drugs. CNM, such as GO, have shown to improve drug uptake into the tumour cells. Also, in vivo assays with CNM evaluated mainly PEG-functionalized graphene oxide, demonstrating a significant decrease in tumour weight and/or volume, while the use of fullerenes and ND only resulted in inhibition of tumour recurrence. When used together with NIR irradiation, SWCNT demonstrated to present a more significant effect leading to cancer cells death, comparing with the other nanomaterials. In general, all CNM above discussed stimulate pro-inflammatory activation, through the production of IFN-γ (mainly induced by MWCNT) and ILs (induced by GQD), favorable to fight against cancer cells, being highlighted lymphomas and carcinomas treatment. That ability makes them potential new tools to be used for cancer treatment and immunotherapy.

Immunomodulation comprises any modification in the immune response to a certain stimulus which can include an enhancement, an induction or a suppression of any segment or of the totality of the immune response. Normally, this effect is generated by an immunomodulator, including biomolecules (e.g. proteins and amino acids), drugs (natural or synthetic) and others substances (e.g. nanomaterials), which can be either an immunostimulating agent or an immunosuppressor [112,113]. Immunomodulatory therapies go much beyond cancer immunotherapy, which was discussed in the previous section. The use of CNM for immunomodulatory therapies targeted to other disorders, such as infectious, auto-inflammatory and autoimmune diseases, where immunoactivation or suppression are required, are discussed below.

Similar to anti-cancer vaccines discussed above, immunomodulatory agents can be used as vaccine adjuvants for the treatment of infectious diseases, like tuberculosis and malaria, and viral infections like foot-and-mouth disease, or Covid19. The attenuated pathogen or part of the pathogen, or its genetic material, can be used as antigens, so a future infection by the live pathogen can be met with a strong and specific immune response. CNM structure and composition may be engineered to better stimulate the immune response. CNM are considered foreign substances to the host organism and are recognized by the immune system triggering a natural response to fight and eliminate them. CMN can induce cells to secrete ROS, consequently stimulating an inflammatory reaction. Due to their small size, CNM can also be phagocyted, mainly by macrophages, and trigger ROS secretion, cell activation and cytokine production (TNF-α, IFN- γ and others). Also, antibodies can be attached to the nanomaterials to target them towards antigen-presenting cells, like macrophages, DC, and B cells. Several studies have demonstrated that CNM, including CNT, GO, r-GO, GQD, fullerenes and ND may have immunostimulatory capabilities, stimulate T-cell response as a consequence of surface functionalization and/or conjugation with drugs, acting as delivery vehicles [13,114–116]. Table 3 . summarizes the main results of most relevant current studies addressing the effect of CNM as immunostimulatory agents, and Fig. 6 . summarizes the main immunostimulatory pathways triggered by CNM.

Flores et al. developed a macrophage therapy using PEG-functionalized SWCNT (4 nM) loaded with an inhibitor of the anti-phagocytic CD47-SIRPα signalling axis, SHP1i. In vitro, SWNT-SHP1i tend to accumulate in murine macrophages and presented an increase of apoptotic cells by macrophages (13,4%), comparing to free SHP1i (6,31%) and SWNT-Cy5.5 controls (5,98%). In vivo, they have shown to reduce the expression of inflammatory genes related to the response to IL-1 and IFN-γ, presenting no mortality with SWNT-SHP1i treatment [117]. Zeinali et al. produced SWCNT conjugated with tuberculin purified protein derivative (PPD) to evaluate a new potential treatment for tuberculosis, directly associated to the activation of T-helper type I response. In vitro, both free SWCNT and PPD-conjugated SWCNT induced an increase in ROS levels on macrophages which is an indicator of an inflammatory response, and a decrease in cell viability. BALB/c mice immunized with SWCNT-PPD presented higher levels of IFN-γ production than those immunized with non-conjugated PPD. This means an increase in the IFN-γ/IL-5 ratio which represents a more pro-inflammatory environment, without cytotoxic effects when comparing with only PPD control [118]. Ovalbumin was coupled to acid functionalized SWCNT in order to study antigens uptake by B cells. It was observed that B cells incubated with OVA-SWCNT conjugates internalized more ovalbumin than B cells incubated with free ovalbumin and a 20% increase of antibody response to OVA-SWCNT was observed, in comparison to OVA alone [119]. Pantarotto et al. functionalized CNT with a peptide from the foot-and-mouth disease virus (FMDV) and OVA, seeking effective ways of administering protective antigens. Results of the mono-derivatized CNT conjugate showed a high stimulation of anti-peptide antibody responses in mice, setting off the possibility to use them in vaccine release for the application in the foot-and-mouth disease [120]. Using a whole-genome expression technology, Pescatori et al. investigated the effect of MWCNT functionalization and diameter on Jurkat and THP1 cell lines, which represented the adaptive and innate immune response, respectively. For this purpose, both cell lines were treated with four different types of functionalized MWCNT (ox-MWCNT-1,
ox − MWCNT − NH
3 +
− 1
,
ox − MWCNT − NH
3 +
− 2
and ox-MWCNT-2). The
ox − MWCNT − NH
3 +
- 2 type affected the regulation of both monocytes and T cells by increasing IL-6, IL-1β and TNF-α production. Also, ox-MWCNT-1 and
ox − MWCNT − NH
3 +
-1 induced IL-10 production in human primary monocytes, and an increase in CCL4L1, IL-1B and TNFAIP6 expression was reported in the presence of MWCNT, suggesting an activation of monocytes and inflammation signalling pathways. It is possible to conclude that the inflammatory response can be related to the oxidation and/or 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition that MWCNT suffer. These types of functionalization may induce the generation of ROS that, as seen above, is a strong inflammatory mediator. However, the low diameter (9.5 nm) and the lack of ammonium groups, verified in the ox-MWCNT-2 type implies the absence of immunostimulatory properties comparing to the other species [121]. Crescio et al. evaluated the capacity of oxidized MWCNT to incite an immune response in vitro, under stimulated microgravity using a Random Positioning Machine. Authors tested primary monocytes and T cells and verified a significant increase of CD69 expression, as well as an enhancement of cytokine production, such as TNF-α, IL-10, IL-13, IFN-γ and IL-2, demonstrating the role of oxidized MWCNT in controlling microgravity-induced immune cells dysregulation (Fig. 7. A) [122]. Furthermore, Yandar et al. conjugated functionalized MWCNT withN-terminus apical membrane antigen 1 (AMA-1) - peptide 21267 - derived from Plasmodium vivax and internalized it in Plasmodium berghei infected mice, to be used in the therapy for malarial infection. Results showed late infection development on the groups immunized with peptide 21267 and MWCNT+peptide 21267, and a greater survival time with the complex compared to controls, although the parasitaemia (average number of parasites in blood) levels were increased. (Fig. 7. B) [123].

Mukherjee et al. studied the influence of GO with different dimensions over cytotoxicity and immune response using primary human monocyte-derived macrophages. GO showed a time-dependant effect over the release of cathepsin B and K+ efflux, being the smaller GO faster in this process. Also, a stimulation of caspase-dependant IL-1β expression was observed in lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-primed macrophages in the presence of GO. LPS are a component of Gram negative bacteria which stimulates TLR4 pathways, resulting in the production of inflammatory factors [124]. Chen et al. investigated the potentiality of GO to induce immune response in macrophages through TLR pathways, which are associated to inflammatory responses. They found a concentration-dependent increase of LC3+ autophagosomes, which suggests an enhancement of autophagy. Furthermore, macrophages treated with GO manifested to be regulated by the TLR4 and TLR9 pathways, producing cytokines, such as IL-2, IL-10, IFN- γ and TNF- α, important factors in the activation of the adaptive immune response [125]. A relevant factor that influences the use of GO as an immunoadjuvant is surface functionalization. Yan et al. studied the potential of preparing GO functionalized with chitosan (CS) as an immunological stimulant and found high viability (∼100%) after culturing RAW264.7 cells for 24 h with this complex, comparing to GO without functionalization. CS-GO stimulated cytokine production, namely IL-6 and TNF-α, comparing to GO alone, supporting the selection of an appropriate surface coating toward enhancing immune cell response [126].

Xu et al. conjugated the immunomodulating peptide tuftsin with the fullerene C60, creating NH2-tuftsin-C60 and C60-tuftsin-COOH. The first (NH2-tuftsin-C60) was conjugated in a solution phase, while in C60-tuftsin-COOH, a pre-functionalized fullerene was covalently attached to tuftsin in a solid-phase. Comparing to natural tuftsin alone, the conjugates slightly increased macrophages phagocytosis and MHC-II expression, without toxicity and manifesting to completely resist to enzymatic hydrolysis [127]. Some years later, the same group investigated the possibility of using fullerenol as a vaccine adjuvant by conjugating it with DNA encoding the HIV-1 Env protein, which is the only surface-expressed viral protein in HIV-1. They found an increase in CD3+ T cell proliferation with the complex Fullerenol-Env in vitro. In mice, after immunization (three times at two-week intervals), it enhanced cellular immunity showing an increase of TNF-α and IFN-γ and all mice survived after 45 days from the treatments beginning (Fig. 8 ) [128].

A different approach was taken by Eidi et al. using fluorescent ND further functionalized by hyperbranched polyglycerol and coupled to Alhydrogel® (AluDia®) to study the possibility of building aluminium-based vaccines without causing neurological autoimmune/inflammatory reactions. The injection of AluDia® was followed in vivo by the presence of fluorescent ND and was detectable in the injected muscle and other organs, like draining lymph nodes, spleen and brain, overcoming the long-term neurotoxicity verified in other organic fluorophores. Results showed a promotion of autophagy, with 71% of granulomas disappearing and a decrease in the size of existing granulomas [129].

CNM impact on immunostimulation has been reported for a diversity of applications, from vaccination against AIDS, to the treatment of infectious diseases like tuberculosis and malaria, or immune system dysregulations. CNM exhibit a small size which facilitates their uptake by cells, thereby stimulating an immune response. For instance, CNT present a high surface area which allows the functionalization with oxygen functional groups, providing stability and allowing their cellular uptake [86–88,121,122]. CNT also manifest to induce ROS generation, which can lead to the activation of an inflammatory response immune cells, contributing to the production of several pro-inflammatory cytokines. These immunostimulatory actions can be very advantageous in the treatment of infectious diseases, like tuberculosis. Size and concentration-dependant GO actions are noticed in in vitro studies described above, to fight against infectious agents. GO manifest to have a time-dependant effect on the cathepsin B release, being observed a faster process with ∼ 350 nm size GO comparing to larger GO particles (∼ 2.4 µm), since nanometric sizes are ideal for cellular uptake. This time- and size-dependant reaction has also shown to trigger inflammasome activation (NLRP3), which is directly related to caspase 1 release and, consequently, the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines. GO presented no toxicity for concentrations equal or inferior to 100 µg mL−1. Functionalized with chitosan, GO revealed to stimulate TLRs pathways and consequently, the inflammatory response, although this is not observed in all the studies analysed [124–126]. Fullerenes and ND also showed positive results, namelythe pro-inflammatory response of fullerenol at concentrations between 20 and 50 µg mL−1, increasing macrophages phagocytosis and CD3 T cells proliferation [127]. ND have shown to increase autophagy replicating the behaviour of common aluminium-based antigen vaccines, overcoming their toxicity limitation. In general, the positive results (pro-inflammatory activation) of CNM in this section, are seen for nanoparticles with sizes inferior to 300 nm, comparing to materials with higher sizes. Also, CNM functionalization have shown to improve their effect on immune cells, since it facilitates their uptake and increases stability. As seen above, physicochemical properties change according to CNM size and surface composition, being important to take these factors in account in order to make possible to adequate/engineer their properties for active immunostimulatory responses to the target diseases.

The inhibition of the immune response, or immunosuppression, can be beneficial in the treatment of inflammatory and autoimmune diseases and allergies, since these pathologies entail an exacerbated immune response, that often causes damage to the affected organs or tissues. Nonetheless, current immunosuppressive therapies, particularly those administered systemically, can lead to greater susceptibility to infections by pathogens, or tumour development. CNM have been reported to have anti-inflammatory properties since they can decrease the levels of oxygen reactive species through the downregulation of the signalling pathways involved [130,131]. This can occur due to specific functionalization with immunosuppressors (drugs or other molecules) [120,132]. On the other hand, MWCNT and fullerenes present a direct influence on immunomodulation [133]. For instance, fullerenes (C60) have the capacity to scavenge free oxygen radicals in their cage structure, reducing the ROS generation. This effect can lower the release of inflammatory and/or allergic mediators from mast cells and basophils from peripheral blood [134]. Also, CNT have been reported to induce specific suppression of the immune system, namely on antibody release and production, and in NK cells activity [133]-[135]. Fig. 9 . points out the main pathways of immunosuppression induced by CNM for the above referred target diseases. Table 4 . summarizes the state-of-the-art literature regarding the outcomes of using CNM for immunosuppression purposes.

The immunomodulatory action of MWCNT was studied by Laverny et al. using PBMCs from healthy and allergic donors. Results showed that, upon stimulation with TLR4 and TLR2/TLR3 agonists and culture in the presence of MWCNT, PBMCs from healthy donors secreted increased levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-12/23p40. A T cell mitogen (phytohemagglutinin A) stimulation led to the secretion of IL-2 and IFN-γ, while in allergic patients, allergen-induced IL-5 (pro-inflammatory cytokine) secretion was inhibited. (Fig. 10 . A) Also, the authors noticed that MWCNT can lead to dendritic cell differentiation [130].

Mitchell et al. tested the effect of MWCNT inhalation (from 0.3 to 5 mg/m3) in systemic/pulmonary immune response. A significant repression of T-cell activity and, consequently, antigen-dependant response in the presence of MWCNT was observed after 14 days of exposure. Also, inhalation of 1 mg m−3 of MWCNT led to a decrease in NK cell activity, supporting that this nanomaterial has the potential to be used as an immunosuppressive vector. (Fig. 10. B) [136].

A very recent investigation from Su et al. revealed the potential of GO coated titanium (Ti-GO) to present positive immunomodulatory effects in osteogenesis. In fact, Ti-GO (0.25 mg mL−1, 24 h of incubation) induced macrophages activation, pro-inflammatory cytokines secretion and osteogenic genes expression under physiological conditions. However, in previously induced acute inflammatory environment, the same complex (Ti-GO) significantly suppressed pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β), suggesting a role for the surrounding microenvironment in modulating CNM-immune cells interaction and response [131]. López-Dolado et al. implanted 3D scaffolds comprising partially reduced GO in surgery-induced spinal cord injury rat model (lesion model: hemisection at C6) and studied tissue feedback. The presence of rGO-modified scaffolds led to a significant stabilization of the injury through the reduction of early inflammatory responses. Also, new blood vessel formation and regeneration of neuronal axons at the periphery of the rGO scaffolds were noticed. On the other hand, in the control scaffolds without rGO, the quantity of blood vessels was decreased and axons were not found. Interestingly, large numbers of macrophages were found in the environment surrounding rGO scaffolds, pointing out that rGO-based materials can induce a pro-regenerative environment in the spinal cord injury [137].

Norton et al. investigated the potential of a fullerene derivative, C70-tetraglycolate (C70-TGA), for control of allergic asthma. Human mast cells incubated with C70-TGA have shown upregulation of CYP1B gene expression and decreased mediator release. Also, IgE production from B cells was found to be reduced. In vivo results showed a decrease in inflammatory cell numbers and in eosinophil infiltration in the airways with C70-TGA tested on an ovalbumin (OVA) challenge model of asthma. The disease was induced by intraperitoneal sensitization with OVA in a first phase and through three intranasal OVA challenges in a second phase, finalizing with the collection of bronchoalveolar lavage fluid and lung tissues. C70-TGA was added three times through an intranasal way, starting on the day before disease induction. Mice treated with C70-TGA presented a decrease in asthma-related cytokines, like IL-4 and IL-5, reducing inflammation, when compared with non-sensitized control animals [132]. Ryan et al. reviewed the possibility of using fullerenes to inhibit allergic response, namely by suppressing the antigen-induced type I hypersensitivity. In vitro tests with human mast cells and peripheral blood basophils showed a significant decrease in ROS production. Furthermore, in vivo assays showed a reduction in histamine response in mice treated with C60, in addition to the stabilization of body temperature, an important indicator of possible asthma reduction in mice [138]. Yudoh et al. examined the possibility of using a water-soluble fullerene (obtained by mechanical milling in liquid media with no addition of polar organic solvents), C60, as an inhibitor of arthritis in vitro and in an arthritis rat model. Fibroblasts, lymphocytes and macrophages were incubated with TNF-α and treated with C60. A decrease of production of inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-1β, in response to TNF-α stimulation was reported. In vivo, groups treated with C60 showed a reduction in the arthritis score, namely on synovitis and joints destruction, comparing to control group, without C60 [139]. Saitoh et al. investigated the radical scavenging properties and cytotoxic effects of three types of fullerenes derivatives (LH-F, HH-F and SHH-F), using a human keratinocyte cell line (HaCaT cells) previously damaged by UV radiation. They found a significant increase of cell viability in the presence of HH-F and SHH-F. The last one significantly reduced ROS levels, comparing to untreated damaged cells (by irradiation) [140]. This would decrease the possibility of triggering an inflammatory response in the irradiation-induced injuries environment of human skin. Yamashita et al. showed the immunomodulatory effect of fullerene, C60, in a mouse model of delayed-type hypersensitivity (DTH) induced through methylbovine serum albumin (mBSA). Firstly, in vitro studies with murine splenocytes revealed a decrease in IL-17, a pro-inflammatory cytokine related to the activation of the innate immune response, leading to a more pronounced immunosuppresive environment, which is favourable for hypersensitivy-related diseases. Importantly, in vivo studies demonstrated a significant decrease of IFN-γ and IL-6 levels and increased Treg/CD4Tcell ratio in DTH mice treated with C60, comparing with untreated mice. Thus, despite the increased TNF-α in C60 treated animals, the cytokine regulation and attenuation of disease led the authors to conclude that C60 was immunomodulatory [141].

Volarevic et al. investigated the effect of large GQD (40 nm) in a model of concanavalin A-related liver damage. GQD injection (50 mg kg−1 of body weight, given 30 min before Con A injection) elicited a protective effect against Con A hepatoxicity, comparing to control without GQD. The authors reported that particularly Jurkat T cells showed to produce higher quantities of IFN-γ. GQD internalization by Jurkat T cells, J774 macrophages and HepG2, a human hepatoma cell line, was observed after 1 h of incubation and it is presented in Fig. 11
. Reduction of the extracellular signal regulated kinase phosphorylation was also noticed in vitro, mainly in Jurkat and J774 cells. Additionally, a decrease in cytotoxicity was observed for HepG2 cells. Reduced IFN-γ production was observed in mouse splenocytes that were isolated, stimulated in vitro with 5 μg mL−1 Con A, and incubated for 24 h with GQD. In vivo results showed accumulation of these nanomaterials in mouse liver, after intravenous injection, as well as the presence of less significant damage and diminished inflammation [142] Kong et al. reported that CD can act as ROS scavengers, preventing related oxidative damage and pro-inflammatory response. Additionally, it was demonstrated that the CD can reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines secretion in vitro, namely TNF- α, and reduce oxidative damage in vivo [143]. A new platform for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis was presented by Pentecost et al. taking advantage of the immunomodulatory capability of ND. Mice were injected with collagen type II to induce arthritis and treated with small quantities (15 µg mL−1) of octadecylamine-functionalized nanodiamond (ND-ODA) and dexamethasone-adsorbed ND-ODA (ND-ODA-Dex). Treated mice presented a relevant reduction of the arthritic score in both ND-ODA and ND-ODA-Dex (similar results) comparing to non-treated mice, as well as a small increase in bone volume after 14 days. Additionally, low doses (15 µg mL−1) of ND-ODA and ND-ODA–Dex led to a reduction in macrophage infiltration and in TNF-α expression, which suggests that ND can be used as an advantageous immunomodulatory platform without the need of dexamethasone, an anti-inflammatory agent [144].

In recent years, interest in new strategies using carbon-derived nanomaterials to harness the power of the immune response, has been mounting. Different materials and modifications continue to be explored for achieving immunosuppression. Fullerene derivatives are the most used type of CNM in this area, in concentrations above 500 µM and performing less than 24 h of incubation, since they can reduce the levels of reactive oxygen species by macrophages due to their ability to scavenge free radicals in their structure. Functionalization of fullerenes like, TGA and hydroxylation, provided them more stability. Also, MWCNT (around 100 µg mL−1, after 48 h of incubation) can indirectly inhibit the immune response since they can activate specific pathways that lead to the suppression of some cytokines production (e.g., IFN- γ). This led to the consequent modulation of the immune response, and resulted in improvements in different disorders models, like asthma and arthritis. Other CNM (GO, rGO, GQD and ND), as seen above, present immunosuppression capabilities, since they have shown to reduce various inflammatory mediators, like TNF-α. However, comparing with fullerenes and MWCNT, their immunosuppressive capabilities deserve to be further investigated. CNM as immunomodulatory materials can provide defined model systems of delivery of immunosuppressive biomolecules, for specific targets, which brings new perspectives to the field of immunotherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overview on translational challenges and clinical concerns
ce:section.ce:para: A safe utilization of CNM, like other nanomaterials, requires a detailed understanding of their interaction with the immune system. Clinical application of nanomedicines in combination with immunotherapy has more than 2 decades, nanocarriers can reduce toxicity and immunosuppressive effects of anti-cancer drugs and improve targeting of anti-cancer vaccines [145]. For instance, Doxil (PEGylated liposome-combined doxorubicin), which promotes direct cancer cell death, but also has immunomodulatory effects, namely in immunogenic cell death, was the first anti-cancer nanomedicine approved by FDA (1995). Also, Abraxane, albumin NPs with paclitaxel, decreased toxicity and increased the time to tumour progression. Both Doxil and Abraxane have been combined with immunotherapeutic molecules (e.g., PD-1/L1 blocking antibodies). The Abraxane combination (with atezolizumab) has got FDA approval, for advanced metastatic triple-negative breast cancer, improving the median progression-free survival. Several clinical trials are ongoing for Doxil combinations with anti-PD-1/L1 antibodies. Also, mRNA-based anti-cancer vaccines are still impaired by enzymatic liability and poor cell entry. Thus, their encapsulation in suitable carriers is of great interest for cancer immunotherapy [145]. Several clinical trials are ongoing using different lipid carriers, with promising results. To achieve the best combinations of nanocarriers and drugs/molecules for different patients and tumours, their biological mechanisms need to be understood, and appropriate biomarkers and patient stratification are required. CNM immunomodulatory properties and capacity to respond to external stimuli will be advantageous in combination with anti-cancer drugs and immunotherapeutic molecules. One of the main concerns that needs to be overcome before clinical application is related to toxicity [146]. This is particularly relevant for patients with chronic diseases and requiring repeated treatments. The toxic effect of nanomaterials on humans is specific to the material, its size and structure [147]. In many cases, in vivo toxicity is the main challenge for material application on the biomedical field and meticulous research in this topic is needed to overcome this problem. For instance, the length of CNT can be related to a strong toxicity since longer ones can provoke an intense inflammatory response, which if uncontrolled will be deleterious. The inhalation of MWCNT can trigger inflammation in the lungs. A recent report indicates that acute lung inflammation is mediated by neutrophils and later involves natural pro-resolving mediators and the macrophage population transition from M1 to M2 phenotypes, in a long resolution phase [148]. Additionally, a rigid type of MWCNT (MWCNT-7) can provoke carcinogenic effects [149,150]. Thorough investigations are needed to understand the limitations in terms of structure, size and concentrations, as it is possible to engineer CNM in order to enhance their biocompatibility and degradation in the human body [151–153].

There are very few studies about the long-term toxicity related to the accumulation of CNM in the organism. Most of the investigations reveal toxicity related to CNT size, as well as dose-dependant toxicity in the case of both G and CNT. The dose-dependant toxicity, in the majority of studies, depends on the material used, the cell or animal models and on the final application [154–157]. In recent years, several materials have been designed for biomedical applications, targeting specific tissues/cells and thus far the long-term risk associated to the accumulation of these compounds in the body is not fully determined. Nevertheless, several studies demonstrated that most CNM referred above are easily biodegraded by various chemical and enzymatic reactions. For instance, pre-treated CNT with sodium hypochlorite, NaClO, and hydrogen peroxide, H2O2, are degraded by oxidation, resulting in large carbon particles agglomerates [25,158]. GO can be degraded by neutrophils through degranulation leading to non-toxic products. Also, photo-Fenton reaction (oxidation) can degrade GO, forming mainly GQD, which are sooner or later decomposed in CO2 and water [159,160]. GO, GQD and fullerenes have been shown to be degraded by a catalysis mediated by human myeloperoxidase, which is produced by neutrophils [24],[161]. Other studies demonstrate that CD have a good biodistribution in mice and successful excretion through urine. Both GQD and CD usually present no toxicity due to their small size and easy elimination [38].

In the last years there were only a few clinical trials involving the use of CNM (e.g., NCT03376984, NCT02698163, NCT02123407). This can be explained with the fact that several of the most recent and promising ones only having started to be explored for biomedical applications less than 10 years ago. Therefore, despite the high potential of CNM to be used as biomaterials, namely in the scope of this review, as colloids for immunotherapy, further study is needed to set the foundations for their translation to clinics [162].

Comparing to traditional therapies, like chemotherapy for cancer treatment, immunotherapy has shown several advantages which can be more effective with the conjugation of both approaches [163,164]. The multidrug resistance is one of the most important barriers in the treatment of several tumours. The use of CNM in cancer immunotherapy can reduce the need for drugs in the treatment, since it modulates the immune system to fight against the threat, decreasing associated side effects, the symptoms and the evolution of the disease. Also, CNM's ability to deliver drugs or small therapeutic molecules to the tumour cells, can increase the immunotherapeutic potential and reduce the side effects [165]. In hypersensitivity cases, CNM based immunotherapy induces long-term allergen-specific tolerance, something that does not happen with pharmacotherapy, which consists in short-time symptom- relieving therapy. The use of immunotherapy reduces the need of constant administration of pharmacological adjuvants [166,167]. With a one-stand use of immunotherapy, the positive results are immediate and last longer, making the treatment cheaper and more effective, since the commonly used pharmacotherapy, normally stands life-long and it only avoids the progression of the disease. Immunotherapy can also prevent disease progression and even provide the complete eradication of the problem, something that is not possible with the use of drugs only [168,169].

CNM have high potential to target the immune response and modulate it towards immunity or tolerance, for different therapeutic outcomes. Although side effects are always present, scientists continue to study new mechanisms to minimize them, maximizing the synergistic effects between drugs and their carriers, and reducing concerns related to toxicity, drug resistance and adverse consequences [170].

The use of adequate pre-clinical models is crucial to understand the mechanisms of action, the targeted delivery of drugs and the safety of the treatments. Though the commonly used rodent models do not always translate the real context of the human immune response, they are only an approximation [171],[172]. Substantial differences exist between the immune system of research laboratory animals and the human species, namely in immune cell differentiation, proliferation and function, the immune system complexity, recognition and activation as well as many other characteristics that need to be considered in order to choose the most adequate animal model. Choosing the right model is a crucial factor in a preclinical evaluation of new therapies to understand the biological mechanisms and reduce risks before clinical trials [173,174]. Particularly, interactions between CNM and immune cells depend on several factors, like structure, dimensions, functionalization and others. The same nanomaterial can activate an immune response in one application and a simple modification on its structure can lead to immune suppression. Although parameters like toxicity can be evaluated in vitro, with good in vitro-in vivo correlation, sometimes the functional impact in vitro, does not translate to in vivo, since factors like the microenvironment, which influence immune activation are more complex, when comparing to the in vitro controlled settings [175,176]. The interaction between various types of immune cells and other cell types of the human body is crucial, and very complex, being many times dependant on the surrounding environment. Consequently, it is exceptionally difficult to replicate in vitro those in vivo interactions [173]. A tumour, for instance, emerges due to somatic mutations in the cell genome, that lead to malignant cell transformation and uncontrolled cell proliferation. Importantly, interactions between these malignant cells and the surrounding milieu, including immune cells, can stimulate tumour growth and development [177,178]. The tumour microenvironment is a critical factor that influences tumour progression, invasion and metastatic capacity, as well as the immune response to the tumour. Therefore, the challenge is to understand the specific-tissue immunity and the constraints to an appropriate therapy [179].

CNM are known to incite a response by the immune system. Depending on their properties and conjugation with different drugs, they are able to trigger immunosuppression and/or immunostimulation, which can be very positive to the development of new nanoscale systems for immunotherapy.

In this review, we discussed the many CNM properties influencing the immune response, such as particle size, surface coating, functionalization and others. Due to their size, CD, CNT and G derivatives are easily internalized by cells making them great vehicles for delivery of therapeutics, including drugs or other biomolecules. In general, CNT have good absorption in the near-infrared region, which makes them important tools in targeted cancer photothermal therapy and immunotherapy. Surface functionalization of GO and GQD allows them to circulate in the body and can also enable specific targeting of molecules/antigens, in order to activate a selective immune response. The induction of reactive oxygen species as a consequence of an activation of the immune cells is very important to stimulate an inflammatory reaction. On the other side, fullerene's structure allows the reduction of those oxidative species, inhibiting the release of inflammatory factors, which can be very useful in inflammatory or allergic diseases. These CNM, as presented, are explored as tools for cancer and autoimmune diseases treatment, namely, as target and drug delivery systems, and vaccine adjuvants or carriers. However, there are still possible side effects associated to consider, as well as the difficulty to translate the in vitro/ in vivo (rodent) results for a human application. Several studies need to be made to evaluate all the conditions that make the same nanomaterial, in specific conditions, an immunosuppressor and, in other circumstances, an immunostimulator. Also, information related to long-term toxicity is still missing since the use of CNM in the field of immunotherapy is quite novel.

Combinations of CNM and drugs/antibodies are still in earlier stages (in vitro assays of toxicity, evaluation of immune cells response in vitro and in vivo). The results so far are very promising, immunotherapy is already seen as a solution and CNM can play a role in directing immune cells, but further investigation is essential to achieve their full potential and revolutionize the future of medicine. Nonetheless, in the last years there were only a few clinical trials involving the use of CNM, therefore further study is needed to set the foundations for their translation to clinics.
